old_claim.note: Advanced Semiconductor Packaging 2.5D/3D is modeled as a necessary component or method for this technology in the current graph.

new_claim.note: Through-silicon vias are used in 2.5D/3D interconnect reference flows, but broad advanced packaging also includes approaches such as fan-out and wafer bonding.

source_shape.source_locator: Abstract describing 2.5D and 3D reference flows employing via-mid copper TSV processing, wafer thinning, backside processing, and chip-to-chip bonding.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The NIST-hosted book chapter describes TSV processing as one element used in 2.5D/3D interconnect reference flows, alongside thinning, backside processing, and bonding.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports the corrected direction because TSVs are a packaging/interconnect technique used by some advanced packaging flows, not a broad packaging category that TSV fabrication depends on.

ontology_before: The graph made TSV fabrication depend on the broad advanced-packaging category, turning a technique into a child that required its own application area.
ontology_after: The graph now treats TSVs as an enabling technique for 2.5D/3D advanced packaging while keeping TSV fabrication grounded in lithography and plasma etching.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Invariant preserved: TSVs remain connected to 2.5D/3D packaging and to fabrication-process prerequisites. Changed: the edge direction now points from the broader packaging application toward the TSV technique.
would_reject_if: A source showed through-silicon vias cannot be defined or fabricated outside the broad advanced-packaging category.
A source showed every technology covered by the broad advanced packaging node requires TSVs as a hard prerequisite.
A future graph split advanced packaging into TSV-based and non-TSV packaging nodes and moved this edge only to the TSV-based node.
short_reason: TSVs are an enabling 2.5D/3D packaging technique, not a technology that hard-depends on the broad advanced-packaging category.
